He always acts weird when girls are around

Personality = how you consistently act across different situations (historical pattern of behavior)

He always acts weird when girls are around = Personality trait
He is timid = Identifying label

posts.title: If you're on your last dollar and struggling to get customers, you need to raise prices, not lower them. If customers are scarce, you have to make them count. Do WAYYYY more for them. And charge WAYYY
posts.content: If you're on your last dollar and struggling to get customers, you need to raise prices, not lower them. If customers are scarce, you have to make them count. Do WAYYYY more for them. And charge WAYYY MORE to do it.

posts.title: The hardest (bootstrapped) revenue level is $1m to $3m in revenue. Main reason: assuming 25% margins, you have $250k to $750k in profit. Typically you need higher value teammates to scale. And they co
posts.content: The hardest (bootstrapped) revenue level is $1m to $3m in revenue. Main reason: assuming 25% margins, you have $250k to $750k in profit. Typically you need higher value teammates to scale. And they cost...$250k-$750k. So you have to bet everything on an unsure thing. 3 Alternatives: 1) Narrow avatar to more valuable customers, so you can make more money with same infra. 2) Make a better offer you can charge more money for 3) Raise prices. But no matter which path you choose, it takes more risk to get out of "the swamp' than it take to get into it (which is why most get in, and never break out).
posts.author: AlexHormozi
posts.summary: 収益100万ドルから300万ドルへの壁とその乗り越え方
posts.mind_theme: ビジネス成長における壁の乗り越え方
key_insights: 100万ドルから300万ドルの収益レベルが最も困難な理由（高価値なチームメンバーのコスト）
この壁を乗り越えるには「より価値の高い顧客層に絞る」「より良いオファーを作る」「価格を上げる」のいずれかが必要
困難を乗り越えるには、入る時以上のリスクが必要である

posts.title:
The reason so many very successful founder have a ‘touch of the tism’ is because what makes you more successful as a child makes you less successful as an adult.

To win as a child you need to follow
posts.content:
The reason so many very successful founder have a ‘touch of the tism’ is because what makes you more successful as a child makes you less successful as an adult.

To win as a child you need to follow rules and get along with everyone to fit in.

To win as an adult you need to write your own rules, get along with few people, and differentiate.

So if you’re split between two paths: conventional wisdom and your own path - pick your own. 

You’ll either be very right or very wrong.

But no one wins big following the herd.

集団に従うだけでは大きな成功は得られない。自分の直感を信じ、独自の道を選ぶべき

posts.title:
You will help more people by trying to make money than you will by staying broke.

When you insist on “impact” you only virtue signal because learning the game is harder than saying you’re “choosing t
posts.content:
You will help more people by trying to make money than you will by staying broke.

When you insist on “impact” you only virtue signal because learning the game is harder than saying you’re “choosing to make less to help people”. 

You’re not helping more people. You’re just less skilled with an ego that prevents you from admitting it.

posts.title: One of the most profound behavior changes I've had as an entrepreneur, which was against my nature, was to stop seeking out opportunities when I still had problems I needed to fix within my business.
posts.content:
One of the most profound behavior changes I've had as an entrepreneur, which was against my nature, was to stop seeking out opportunities when I still had problems I needed to fix within my business. 

You have to switch from shiny object syndrome to golden BB obsession. 

Fixing a hundred small things is more boring but creates the outsized outcomes the shiny object syndrome promises but never delivers.

多くの小さな問題を修正することが、大きな成果を生み出す

Buy things that other people misjudge as risky.

posts.content:
How to make mega money:

Buy things that other people misjudge as risky.

Ex:
Quitting your job.
Moving across the country. 
Going all in.

The reality is that the alternative is riskier - it just takes longer to realize it.

posts.content:
5 Steps to solving 99% of sales issues:
1) Actually have an offer not just "buy my thing"
2) Have a written script
3) Practice the script daily (don't allow off script sales)
4) Respond to leads immediately
5) Sell to richer customers

Actually do them rather than nod along.

posts.title:
If you make content, some people will find a way to hate you for it. 

This is a cost of doing business. Reminder: almost every major prophet was murdered - public sentiment is a poor short term gauge
posts.content:
If you make content, some people will find a way to hate you for it. 

This is a cost of doing business. Reminder: almost every major prophet was murdered - public sentiment is a poor short term gauge for human value (See: Jesus).

1) People create straw men: "if you tell me I should focus on long term bets you're telling me I should NEVER enjoy my life"

2) You will become more interesting than smaller creators, and negative hooks create drama, so bad creators will make negative hooks about you because they don't know how to gather attention on their own.

3) Media is consumed in short snippets. 99% of people will never consume more than one angle - then derive an almost entirely self-generated opinion of you. This means most people will literally never understand you. IT also means that the praise and boos have equivalent value - zero.

TLDR: You cannot demand people have infinite context in a world that offers none.

posts.title: People will spend a 1000 hours trying to make passive income and those same 1000 hours put to making active income would make them more than the passive income would ever make over the rest of their l
posts.content:
People will spend a 1000 hours trying to make passive income and those same 1000 hours put to making active income would make them more than the passive income would ever make over the rest of their lives.

The people who have the most passive income start with high active income.

You've gotta have stuff to invest first - and - the ability to lose as you learn.

Don't believe the lie. A few get lucky on passive income schemes. But no one gets rich. 
Except for the one promoting them (which is their active income - surprise).

posts.title: Life advice nobody told you: Talent and intelligence are overrated. Intelligent people are more likely to overthink, overplan, and overanalyze. They hide behind motion that doesn't create progress. Th
posts.content: Life advice nobody told you: Talent and intelligence are overrated. Intelligent people are more likely to overthink, overplan, and overanalyze. They hide behind motion that doesn't create progress. They fear the judgment of others if they're proven wrong. The truth is that talent and intelligence are abundant. Courage is not. The people you admire are the ones who had the courage to act. They aren’t more talented than you. They aren’t smarter than you. They just took action when you didn’t. I often wonder how many extraordinary people wasted their entire lives waiting for permission that never came. Permission isn't granted. It's taken. You get to tap yourself in whenever you want. You can just do things.
posts.author: SahilBloom
posts.summary: 才能や知性は過大評価されている。重要なのは行動する勇気だ。
posts.mind_theme: 成功に必要な行動力と勇気のマインドセット
key_insights: 才能や知性は豊富だが、それらが過度な思考や分析につながり、行動を妨げることがある。

posts.title: Career advice nobody told you: You have to demonstrate excellence in everything you do. Your income scales proportional to the amount of excellence that you're able to demonstrate. Strategic incompete
posts.content: Career advice nobody told you: You have to demonstrate excellence in everything you do. Your income scales proportional to the amount of excellence that you're able to demonstrate. Strategic incompetence is a lie. You don't get to pick and choose when to show up, because the world will ignore your best and judge you for your worst. Everything matters. Every single thing. Top performers show up with energy and enthusiasm for the little things just as much as they do for the big things.  If you're in the top-10% of performers, there's no ceiling for what you can do. But the self-awareness to identify where you currently stack up, and adapt to the honest feedback on it, is very rare.  If you're in the top-10%, you know it. If you're not, figure out why and fix it.

posts.title: Nobody told you this about success: Rent is due every single day. A lot of people seem to think that after you make it you can coast in the idyllic land of success. This is wrong. Every single day, yo
posts.content: Nobody told you this about success: Rent is due every single day. A lot of people seem to think that after you make it you can coast in the idyllic land of success. This is wrong. Every single day, you have to fight to earn your seat at the table. And that fight gets more intense as you have more success. You have more to lose. More mouths to feed. More people counting on you. More expectations. There's an old saying that I love: Every morning in the savannah, the gazelle wakes up and knows it must outrun the lion or be killed. The lion wakes up and knows it must outrun the gazelle or starve. Whether you're the gazelle or the lion, when you wake up in the morning, you'd better start running. Rent is due daily. Pay it with pride.
posts.author: SahilBloom
posts.summary: 成功は維持が最も難しく、日々努力し続ける必要がある。

posts.title: Money advice nobody told you: The only way to make a lot of money is to create a lot of value. No one hands out money. No one is going to pay you just because they like you or think you're cool. That'
posts.content: Money advice nobody told you: The only way to make a lot of money is to create a lot of value. No one hands out money. No one is going to pay you just because they like you or think you're cool. That's not the way the world works. Money earned is a direct byproduct of value created. Create value, receive value. If money is the goal, value has to be the focus. This isn't just some vague idea: The only way to get rich is to create an enormous amount of value for others, and capture a small portion of that along the way. It's not talking about the thing, it's not brainstorming about the thing, it's not asking about the thing, it's not thinking about the thing. The only way to create value is by doing the thing. And if you don't know where to start, look around you. Customers, colleagues, bosses, shareholders, employees. Every single one of them has a problem. What problems can you solve for the people around you? Figure them out, solve them, scale that solution. That's how you make money.

posts.title: Always take yourself seriously. Stop showing up in the world like it was an accident. Do the little things to show up as the best version of yourself. Move your body daily. Eat real foods. Get good sl
posts.content: Always take yourself seriously. Stop showing up in the world like it was an accident. Do the little things to show up as the best version of yourself. Move your body daily. Eat real foods. Get good sleep. Buy a few outfits that fit. Maintain good hygiene rituals. Move slowly and deliberately. Head tall. Use open body language. Never cross your arms in front of you. None of that needs to be expensive, but not doing it will be. The way the world treats you is a simple reflection of the way you treat yourself. Always carry yourself like your life matters. Because it does.

posts.title: Something I know (but still underestimate): Your environment shapes your entire reality. The people you surround yourself with determine your outcomes. Surround yourself with people who are constantly
posts.content: Something I know (but still underestimate): Your environment shapes your entire reality. The people you surround yourself with determine your outcomes. Surround yourself with people who are constantly talking about the past, you'll be stuck in it. Surround yourself with people thinking big about the future, you'll build a bright one. Spend time with people who encourage you to think bigger. Who believe you’re capable of more. A person is either holding you back or pushing you forward. There is no in between.

posts.title: Whenever I feel stuck, I add structure to my days. Map out what you’re going to do for a day in 30 minute increments. It doesn’t have to be the “right” stuff. It just needs to be something. Then stick
posts.content: Whenever I feel stuck, I add structure to my days. Map out what you’re going to do for a day in 30 minute increments. It doesn’t have to be the “right” stuff. It just needs to be something. Then stick to it for an entire day. You’ll feel in control and create momentum. It works.

posts.title: The life cheat code nobody told you: Be the most interested person in the room. Everyone tries to be the most interesting person in the room. The most compelling stories. The funniest lines. The most
posts.content: The life cheat code nobody told you: Be the most interested person in the room. Everyone tries to be the most interesting person in the room. The most compelling stories. The funniest lines. The most impressive credentials. The most names dropped. Don't be everyone. It backfires. It feels painfully forced. It reads as insecure. Instead, focus on being interested, not interesting. Turn outward. Take a genuine interest in others. Not as a means to an end, but because you actually want to learn about who they are as a person, beneath the surface. When you open up to people, they can feel it. They reciprocate and open up to you. Be visibly happy to see people. Smile at people. Ask high-quality questions. What are you most excited about right now? What's creating the most energy in your life at the moment? What's lighting you up outside of work? Ask follow-up questions. Be glowing about what other people are doing. Not fake or disingenuous, but genuinely excited about what they're excited about. Lighting up for others makes them light up for you. Being interested is how you become interesting.

posts.title:
I call this test my New Opportunity Razor…

1. Do I like the winning version of this thing?

posts.content:
I call this test my New Opportunity Razor…

Here are the two questions to assess any new opportunity:

1. Do I like the winning version of this thing?

Imagine yourself five years in the future. This new thing you’ve taken on is crushing it. You’re up there with the best at it.

Do you like what that looks like as it relates to your life, time, and energy?

In other words, if you make it to the top, are you going to like the view from the summit?

This is a critical first step, because too often in life we climb a mountain for years, get to the top, and realize that we never really wanted the view in the first place.

If you don’t like the life of the person in the corner office, you may want to think about that before you sacrifice 20 years grinding away to get it (or figure out how you’re going to do it differently, at least).

If the answer is no, stop here and say no to the opportunity.

If the answer is yes, proceed to the second question…

2. Am I willing to do the losing version of this thing for a long time?

To earn anything meaningful in life, it’s going to take a long, long time. Probably much longer than even your most optimistic initial assumptions.

An American computer scientist named Douglas Hofstadter once coined the self-referential adage, Hofstadter’s Law, which states:

“It always takes longer than you expect, even when you take into account Hofstadter's Law.”

So, knowing this, do you have energy to do the losing, bad, ugly version of this thing for a long time in order to earn the winning version that you like?

You may dive into something because of the appeal of the summit, but you’ll never make it there if you don’t embrace the mud you have to crawl through on the climb.

The people who have reached those summits have one common trait:

They loved the mud. They obsessed over the details. They had real energy for it.

When asked about how he had sustained his high level play for so long, tennis legend Novak Djokovic had a simple response:

"I can carry on playing at this level because I like hitting the tennis ball."

The summit is the grand slam championships. Standing on center court, holding the trophy high in the air. Everyone likes that version.

But the way you earn it is through thousands upon thousands of hours of hitting the ball. In the cold. In the dark. In the rain. When nobody’s watching. When nobody’s cheering. When nobody cares.

The losing version is the cost of entry for the winning version.

The New Opportunity Razor has been a major life cheat code.

The best opportunities for your life will pass this test.

You'll love the summit and the mud you have to navigate to get there.

When you find those things, go all in. Say no to everything else.

posts.title: Life advice nobody told you: Always default to praise of people who aren't in the room. The way you talk about someone who isn't in the room is one of the loudest signals about the energy you carry. A
posts.content: Life advice nobody told you: Always default to praise of people who aren't in the room. The way you talk about someone who isn't in the room is one of the loudest signals about the energy you carry. An observation about the most impressive, magnetic people I've been around: They never speak badly about people who aren't there. They never use underhanded or discrediting comments. They glow about them, or they say nothing. If everyone is speaking negatively about someone, it's perfectly reasonable to opt out and say nothing. It shifts the entire power balance of the conversation in your favor. It shows a level of stoic awareness and calm that stands out. We all silently catalogue how others speak about people who aren't in the room, because we know that we may be the topic of that conversation as soon as we leave. Those who default to praise command the room and silently set a standard of safety that everyone else can feed off of.

posts.title:
Ted Turner was one of the great entrepreneurs…  

My 10 favorite quotes of his I go back to again and again… RIP Ted

“Life is a game. Money is how we keep score.”

“You cannot be a risk taker without
posts.content:
Ted Turner was one of the great entrepreneurs…  

My 10 favorite quotes of his I go back to again and again… RIP Ted

“Life is a game. Money is how we keep score.”

“You cannot be a risk taker without taking risks.”

“Early to bed, early to rise, work like hell, and advertise.”

“Lead, follow, or get out of the way.”

“If you’re going to have a major impact on the world, you have to be willing to be criticized.”

“Most people think small because they’re afraid to fail.”

“I was willing to bet the company over and over again.”

“The world is run by people who show up.”

“I learned that persistence and determination alone are omnipotent.”

“People who say it cannot be done should not interrupt those who are doing it.”
posts.author: Codie_Sanchez
posts.summary: テッド・ターナーの成功哲学：リスク、行動力、粘り強さ。
posts.mind_theme: 成功者の思考法とビジネス哲学
key_insights: 人生はゲームであり、お金は得点である。
リスクを取らずしてリスクテイカーにはなれない。
早寝早起き、猛烈に働き、宣伝する。
世界を動かすのは、行動する人々である。
粘り強さと決断力だけが全能である。
できないと言う人々は、やっている人々の邪魔をするべきではない。
posts.target_audience: 起業家、ビジネスパーソン、成功哲学を学びたい人
posts.category: マインドセット/思考法
posts.credibility: フォロワー84万人

自己の成長や方向性を決定する上で、周りの人々の影響は大きい。
成功のためには、自分を取り巻く環境を意識的に選ぶことが重要。
posts.target_audience: 自己成長を望む人、人間関係に悩む人

大胆な初期提案と笑顔で対応する姿勢は、交渉を有利に進める上で重要。
文化的な背景を持つ交渉のプロから学ぶことで、ビジネススキルを磨くことができる。

key_insights: 一般的な人々が税金をただ支払うのに対し、富裕層は税制を研究し、賢く対応する。
税金は単なる支払いではなく、戦略的に管理すべき要素である。
税金に関する知識を深めることは、資産形成において極めて重要。

2. Biased towards young and hungry. 
3. 0% remote, 100% in office
4. Work non-stop.
5. No longer bragging about te
posts.content:
The best CEO's and companies right now are:

1. Obsessed w/ the problem for 10 years+
2. Biased towards young and hungry. 
3. 0% remote, 100% in office
4. Work non-stop.
5. No longer bragging about team size.
posts.author: Codie_Sanchez
posts.summary: 優秀なCEOと企業の共通点：課題への執着、若手採用、オフィス勤務など
posts.mind_theme: 成功するCEOと企業の経営哲学
key_insights: 成功するCEOは10年以上一つの問題に執着し続ける。
若くてハングリーな人材を優先的に採用する。
100%オフィス勤務を重視し、リモートワークは行わない。
ノンストップで働き、チームの規模を誇らない。
posts.target_audience: 経営者、リーダーシップを学びたい人、スタートアップ関係者

key_insights: 人生のピークは30代とは限らず、30代後半からでも新たな挑戦で成功できる。
年齢を重ねるごとに経験と知恵が増し、人生はより良い方向に向かう可能性がある。
コンテンツ作成やポッドキャスト開始など、遅咲きの成功例は勇気を与える。

posts.title:
Every (good) business used to have 4 cycles:

1. You do everything.
2. You hire generalists to do many things.
3. You hire specialists to replace generalists.
4. The specialists run everything.

Now i
posts.content:
Every (good) business used to have 4 cycles:

1. You do everything.
2. You hire generalists to do many things.
3. You hire specialists to replace generalists.
4. The specialists run everything.

Now it has 3: 

1. You do everything.
2. You use AI to automate as much as you can 
3. You hire AI-obsessed specialists that know how to leverage the automations

A pattern I've noticed in people who are burned out...

You’re taking too many meetings. If you can’t cancel them, walk during one. If you can’t do that, shorten it by 15 minutes. If you can’t shorten, have an agenda.

Meetings will suck the life out of you.

posts.title:
From Hollywood assistant making $30k/year working 18-hour days to running a $9.6M/year pressure washing business.

It all started when friend called him up to help with his carpet cleaning business wh
posts.content:
From Hollywood assistant making $30k/year working 18-hour days to running a $9.6M/year pressure washing business.

It all started when friend called him up to help with his carpet cleaning business while he was still in his day job.

As you can probably guess, he said yes and worked with him for a few years. But at some point, he was ready to become an owner himself.

His criteria was simple:

-Recession-resistant
-Huge addressable market
-Fragmented industry (lots of small players, no big boys)
-High margins

Pressure washing checked all 4 boxes.

But Aaron didn't start from zero. He found a 34-year-old business already doing $2.2M/year and acquired it.

When I last saw him, he was at:

187 locations nationwide. 1000s of customers. $800k in monthly revenue.

All because a Hollywood assistant picked up the phone when his buddy needed help with a carpet cleaning business.

posts.title:
10 YEARS OF FOUNDER THERAPY, SUMMARIZED IN ONE MINUTE.

1) 𝗡𝗼𝗯𝗼𝗱𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀. Being a founder means accepting that everything in this company is your fault, even the parts that ar
posts.content:
10 YEARS OF FOUNDER THERAPY, SUMMARIZED IN ONE MINUTE.

1) 𝗡𝗼𝗯𝗼𝗱𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀. Being a founder means accepting that everything in this company is your fault, even the parts that aren't. The day you stop blaming is the day you actually start building something.

2) 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺𝘀 𝗱𝗼 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝗳𝗶𝘅𝗲𝗱. The cofounder tension, the cash flow stress, the 3am "is this all going to work" spiral. You don't solve them. You build the nervous system to operate while they're happening. That is the job.

3) 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻 𝗹𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂, 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗹𝘆. It tells you a slow month is the end. It tells you the competitor is winning when they're actually panicking too. It tells you everyone has it figured out except you. Most of the time, the panic is louder than the problem.

4) 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝘁𝗿𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗹 𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗘𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗺𝗼𝘀. 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁𝘆. The fastest way to die is convincing people they need what you're selling. The right customers won't need a pitch deck and a 40 minute call. Everyone else will eat your calendar and still leave a one star review.

5) 𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗱𝗼 𝗶𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗱𝗶𝗲. 𝗬our first business will probably fail. So will most of your second. Most people quit two inches before the gold. Some people should quit two inches before the cliff. Knowing the difference is the whole skill.

6) 𝗢𝗻𝗹𝘆 𝗮 𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗼𝗳 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗴𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗺. One or two cofounders. A mentor who told you the truth when it cost them nothing to lie. When you find them, pay them well, protect their time, and never forget who was there at zero.

posts.title:
Interviewed a candidate yesterday and asked them to map out for me their thought process for building a strategy. 

They said it would be faster with ai, but I wanted to see them think live. Watched t
posts.content:
Interviewed a candidate yesterday and asked them to map out for me their thought process for building a strategy. 

They said it would be faster with ai, but I wanted to see them think live. Watched them visibly shaken, and annoyed at having to do it in real time. 

The person couldn't explain why the strategy worked, the underlying math to support it. This generation is going to (maybe already does?) have cognitive decline from letting AI think for them. Brain is just a muscle.

posts.title:
The modern career path in 2026:

You use AI to write your resume.
HR uses AI to screen it.
You pass a HireVue interview where AI scores your eye contact.
You get hired.
You use AI to do your work.
You
posts.content:
The modern career path in 2026:

You use AI to write your resume.
HR uses AI to screen it.
You pass a HireVue interview where AI scores your eye contact.
You get hired.
You use AI to do your work.
Your boss uses AI to give feedback.
You use AI to write your reports.
Leadership uses AI to summarize those.
Twelve months in, your role gets "restructured" - because a $20/month subscription can do 70% of it.

posts.title:
One of the most overlooked AI opportunities in the next 24 months is voice agents for boring businesses.

Every HVAC, plumbing, and pest control company in America is sending calls to voicemail after
posts.content:
One of the most overlooked AI opportunities in the next 24 months is voice agents for boring businesses.

Every HVAC, plumbing, and pest control company in America is sending calls to voicemail after 5pm. One AI voice agent fixes it overnight. 

Most owners have never even heard of this technology, yet alone know how to implement it. 

The person who packages this up and sells it to 500 of them is going to be very rich...very quietly.

posts.title:
Advice I wish someone gave me:

People will say “you’ve changed” because they don’t know how to say “you’ve grown.” So if you’re looking for permission to outgrow someone or something, this is it. Not
posts.content:
Advice I wish someone gave me:

People will say “you’ve changed” because they don’t know how to say “you’ve grown.” So if you’re looking for permission to outgrow someone or something, this is it. Not everyone will be a part of every chapter of your life. Some will come and go, and that’s okay. If a person, group, or situation is keeping you from continuing to grow and evolve, it’s time to move on from them. However, recognize this will not come easily, and they will actively try to keep you from doing so. Be ready for this.

posts.title: On Tuesday, Coinbase laid off 14% of its staff.
posts.content:
On Tuesday, Coinbase laid off 14% of its staff.                                                                                                                                                                                                                    

But the worst part?

Many of these people were:

• Designers
• Engineers
• Product managers

And other high-level or strategic roles normally "safe" from being replaced by AI.

But Brian (the CEO of Coinbase) was brutally honest:

"Non-technical teams are now shipping production code and many of our workflows are being automated."

He even wants to run one-person teams where a single employee handles:

• Design
• Engineering
• AND product management

Which begs the question:

Will this person be compensated in proportion to the value they create (and save) Coinbase?

Now, I'm no betting man.

So, I wouldn't get my hopes up if I were in that position.

But this is just another (almost weekly) brutal reminder:

You don't owe your employer your loyalty.

Because that loyalty is clearly one-sided.

In Coinbase's case, they'll let you go:

• When the economy is shaky
• When you've given them your best years
• When the price of gas and other everyday items explode

All with little to no hesitation.

There is only ONE safety net you can count on:

Yourself.

There has never been a better time in history to build wealth if you choose to invest in yourself.

Nothing will give you a better return over the course of your life.

(Not even crypto!)

This is why I train people on these in-demand skills:

• Sales
• Writing
• Business
• Marketing
• Offer Creation
• AI Prompt Engineering

No one can take these away from you.

You can start learning these skills TODAY.

And ghostwriting is the perfect vehicle to master all these skills.

Right now, the fastest way to get started is by ghostwriting on X.

So, I put together a complete crash course on how to become an X Ghostwriter.

It has everything you need to get started.

Click here to get (free) instant access: https://t.co/uBPA5fk0fe

AI時代には複数のスキルを統合する能力が求められる
posts.target_audience: 将来のキャリアに不安を感じる人、AIの進化に関心がある人

posts.credibility: フォロワー44万人

posts.title: I wasted over 1,000 hours reading useless books on writing.
posts.content:
I wasted over 1,000 hours reading useless books on writing.                                                                                                                                                           

Instead, I could have saved my time and just read these 9 books:

1. The Adweek Copywriting Handbook by Joseph Sugerman

The only copywriting book you'll ever need – full stop.

Learn the art of capturing & keeping attention.

2. The Boron Letters by Gary Halbert

Read this book for 1 reason:

To study Gary Halbert's incredible writing style.

This guy has an incredible way with words – a voice, rhythm, and tone that keeps your eyes glued to the page.

3. Show Your Work by Austin Kleon

The idea of the writer slaving away for months in solitude is no longer true.

The best writers are sharing all of their ideas in public, getting feedback, and iterating.

4. The War of Art by Steven Pressfield

Writing is a daily battle with the Resistance.

But until you're aware of it, the Resistance will dominate you.

This book single-handedly changed my life.

Once you read it, you'll never see creative work the same way.

5. The Sense of Style by Steven Pinker

This is the best book on grammar and style.

It's a modern take on Strunk & White's classic.

6. On Writing Well by William Zinsser

The no-BS guide to writing effective non-fiction.

Filled with examples of classic mistakes that, once you see, you'll never make again.

7. Bird by Bird by Anne Lamott

This is one of the greatest books on the writing process.

It's a comforting, funny, and practical guide to getting words on the page.

8. The Elements of Style by Strunk & White

The most tactical book on this list.

• Active vs. passive voice
• Positive vs. negative form
• Concise vs. verbose writing
• Lists of words to never use again

Keep this one on your desk at all times.

9. The Art of the Pitch by Peter Coughter

Every writer who wants to make money writing online needs to read this.

This book teaches you how to sell your ideas.

After 5+ years as a digital writer, all writing is selling.

This book will show you how to sell your ideas to clients, bosses, and readers.

10. (BONUS) The Art and Business of Online Writing by Nicolas Cole

This book completely broke my brain about what it meant to write online.

• Why you shouldn't start a blog
• Generating an endless amount of ideas
• The "Golden Intersection" of telling personal stories

Truly epic.

posts.title: No one will tell you this, but you can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation. Every time I face a mental health dip, it’s always when I’v
posts.content: No one will tell you this, but you can solve almost every modern-day problem with writing, exercise, clean eating, sunshine, and meditation. Every time I face a mental health dip, it’s always when I’ve drifted away from these basics. So now, whenever I feel off, I start by checking each of them off in a single day. Then, once I’ve reset my baseline and I’m thinking clearly, the problem I thought I had either disappears or is much easier to solve—because I’m operating from a position of clarity.

posts.title:
7. Writing vs Editing

Writing and editing are different processes:

• Writing: This is all addition. Getting raw material out of your head and onto a page. In this stage, rip out your backspace butto
posts.content:
7. Writing vs Editing

Writing and editing are different processes:

• Writing: This is all addition. Getting raw material out of your head and onto a page. In this stage, rip out your backspace button.

• Editing: This is all subtraction. In this stage, put your backspace key back and rip out everything else.

Copywriting is simple:

posts.title: I hate to break it to you, but 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity. There are only 2 reasons to consume content. 1) It’s providing an answer to a p
posts.content: I hate to break it to you, but 95% of reading, planning, and brainstorming is procrastination disguised as productivity. There are only 2 reasons to consume content. 1) It’s providing an answer to a problem that will remove the current bottleneck in your life or business OR 2) It’s going to inspire you to create more content after you’ve consumed it. If it’s not solving a problem for you or inspiring you, be honest with yourself that it’s not actually moving you forward. Then, get to work doing the work you’re avoiding.

posts.title: I can quickly name a handful of strategic mistakes I’ve made in the last year trying to scale my business:
posts.content:
I can quickly name a handful of strategic mistakes I’ve made in the last year trying to scale my business:                                                                                                                                                                            

- Over-hiring / adding excess capacity too quickly in an effort to scale

- Spending money on unprofitable ad campaigns rather than doubling down on what was working organically

- Running a repeated event too many times rather than coming up with completely new material

- Outsourcing our content production too much rather than continuing to write new things

- Rolling out an internal or external policy change without thinking through literally every pushback scenario

- And on and on

But rather than look at these as “negative” mistakes, I’ve reframed them as a positive.

Because now, I think of failure as getting paid “Mistake Dividends.”

Let me explain:

If you think about making an investment in something, a new initiative, channel, business, team, etc. you can think about it as buying an asset.

And when you buy an asset, two things can happen:

- It can work out. If so, great, you made a good strategic choice

- If it doesn’t work out, you still collect an asset in the form of a mistake

Obviously, the first has a clear positive return.

And if you allow it to, the second scenario, despite being “failure,” can pay you back a positive dividend in multiple ways:

- You can avoid making that mistake again in the future (saving you future time/money)

- You can tell the story of that mistake to other people (like I’m doing now), so hopefully they can avoid it too

- And in the future, you can laugh at how inexperienced or naive you were at various stages of your business journey

All three of these are net positives in the long run. And you only get them if you try something uncertain and it doesn’t work out.

So this reframe helps me take on things with fixed downside, knowing they will pay me in the future one way or another.

posts.title:
If I could tell every 20-year-old in the world 3 things, I would tell them this:

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

Your wealth is a direct measure of the amount of val
posts.content:
If I could tell every 20-year-old in the world 3 things, I would tell them this:

1. No one cares what you do, they care what you can do for them.

Your wealth is a direct measure of the amount of value you create in the world. You have to constantly look for ways to solve other people’s problems. The bigger problems you solve, the more you will make.

2. No one on Earth is thinking about you as much as you think they are.

You are not the center of the universe. People are way too busy thinking about themselves to pay attention to you. And most of the anxious feelings you experience come from thinking you’re more important than you are.

3. Most of the things you are caught up in right now will not matter in 5 years. 

Trust me: future you is looking back and laughing at the things you’re currently caught up in. And if you want proof of this, just look back on what you yourself were caught up in 5 years ago.
posts.author: dickiebush

posts.title:
If you’re like me, your most excited mornings come when you wake up with a COMPLETELY clear calendar.

My output on these days is 50-100% higher on *completely* clear days versus days where I have eve
posts.content:
If you’re like me, your most excited mornings come when you wake up with a COMPLETELY clear calendar.

My output on these days is 50-100% higher on *completely* clear days versus days where I have even one meeting.

Here's the process I use to minimize meetings (while keeping communication high):

But first, there are 3 reasons why I need days with absolutely no meetings:

1. I have full freedom over how I will work that day.

I can be anywhere, take breaks when I need to, spend more time writing when I’m in flow, switch up my schedule, etc.

2. I have zero looming open loops I need to address later.

There’s nothing urgently grabbing at my attention that I have to prepare for or think about solving.

3. I can go fully offline & disappear into deep work.

Since no one is expecting to communicate with me synchronously, I can confidently leave my devices at home knowing any loops can be handled later.

Now, this all sounds great in theory. 

But unless you set intentional rules, meetings naturally find their way on my calendar most days.

So here’s the process you can follow to maximize your meeting-free blocks: 

1. Try to turn as many meetings as possible async.

This alone eliminates 70-80% of them. Run an experiment where you take all of your scheduled meetings and run them asynchronously. You’d be surprised how much other people would prefer it + how little you actually need to be on Zoom.

2. If a meeting is high-leverage, batch it to one day of the week.

For me, that is Monday right now. This helps me string together as many meeting-free days as possible from Tuesday all the way until Sunday.

3. Be rigid in upholding my meeting rules.

It’s tempting to make exceptions or sacrifice your rules for other people’s convenience.

But these rules are useless if you don’t uphold them.

So I’ve created a script you can turn to whenever someone wants to schedule a meeting:

Hey [name], I’m currently heads down in a writing mode, keeping a clear calendar on X-Y days of the week. But I can do X time or Y time on ABC date if that works for you? Otherwise, happy to jam async if it’s more urgent.

I developed these rules from a simple reflection I did on days where I felt happiest & days where I felt most anxious or dissatisfied. 

The days when I had something scheduled that forced me to be in a certain place at a certain time were my days of the most dissatisfaction.

But the days when I had zero meetings and could fully sink into flow on some deep work were the days I was most excited. 

So my goal for the rest of the year is to maximize the number of meeting-free, writing-focused days. 

Hope this helps you do the same ✊

posts.content:
2. There will never be a perfect time to start writing

The "Once I ... then I'll start writing" is the ultimate form of procrastination.

• Once work slows down
• Once I feel like I am ready

The "Onces" will always be there—so get started today.

posts.title: How much can you charge as a writer?
posts.content:
How much can you charge as a writer?                                                                                                                                                                                                    

- $2,000?
- $5,000?
- $10,000?

The answer is:

As much as you want...

Once you have an Irresistible Offer.

For example:

We helped this ghostwriter charge $32,500 for two high-paying projects (with the option for a monthly retainer).

Now, it should go without saying how insane some people will find this.

My first client as a ghostwriter paid me $5,000.

And this just goes to show that the only reason I couldn't have charged more is because I failed to communicate my value clearly.

So I created a free, 5-day email course to help you become a ghostwriter (and land high-paying clients).

Get access here: https://t.co/uBPA5fk0fe

posts.content:
I can point most of the success in my life back to these 5 habits:

1. Walk 15,000 steps per day
2. Take an obsessive amount of notes
3. Write for 90 minutes every morning
4. Wake up at 5 AM and go to bed by 9 PM
5. Cook 99% of my own meals with clean, single-ingredient foods

They worked for me, they might work for you.

posts.title:
Money advice I wish someone gave me sooner:

Don’t touch your phone for the first 60 minutes of the day

Too many people lurch for their dopamine casino within the first few minutes of waking up.

Thi
posts.content:
Money advice I wish someone gave me sooner:

Don’t touch your phone for the first 60 minutes of the day

Too many people lurch for their dopamine casino within the first few minutes of waking up.

This immediately puts them into a reactive state. Other people’s lives, other people’s messages, other people’s needs, all of it hogging your attention before you’re even out of bed.

To avoid this, you’re going to charge your phone in another room.

The staples you need to hit to start your day:

- Walking
- Hydrating
- Journaling
- Exercising

The easiest order to do this is to wake up, immediately hydrate, and sit down with your journal.

Here’s a starter journal prompt:

First, brain dump any ideas that are top of mind.

Then, answer these 3 questions:

- What am I excited about today?
- What am I going to learn today?
- What’s the 1 habit I’m going to avoid today?

This should only take 5-10 minutes

When you’re done journaling, walk to the gym to start your workout.

Within the first 90 minutes of the day, you are completely primed to dominate your work day.

From here you’ll cook breakfast, caffeinate, and post up at your desk ready to get after it.

posts.summary: 成功者が教える朝の習慣：起床後60分間はスマホを触らず自分を優先

key_insights: 起床後60分間はスマホに触れず、ドーパミンカジノを避ける

posts.title:
I have a clear goal for my portfolio of digital writing businesses to hit $10,000,000 in annual revenue this year.

But as an organization, we need to become the versions of ourselves capable of achie
posts.content:
I have a clear goal for my portfolio of digital writing businesses to hit $10,000,000 in annual revenue this year.

But as an organization, we need to become the versions of ourselves capable of achieving this.                  

And the last 3 months have been a brutal reminder:

The standard on your team is whatever you allow to slide without consequence.

A closer skips their pipeline review three days in a row and nobody says anything? That is now the standard.
A department head sends a sloppy EOD report and it gets accepted without comment? New standard.
A setter misses their daily activity number and the week just moves on? Same thing.

Most people say a standard one time and expect it to be upheld. And it is, for the first few days.

Then, it slowly drifts.

50 small reinforcing events in a row, each of which too small to notice in the moment. And 6 months later, you check in and realize performance is not even close to what you expected.

So remember:

At any given time, you are either reinforcing a standard, or letting it slowly erode.

There is no in between.
posts.author: dickiebush

posts.title:
I recently stumbled upon this quote from Frank Slootman (CEO of Snowflake), and I can’t stop thinking about it:

“Mediocrity is the silent killer.

Organizations are not getting killed by their C play
posts.content:
I recently stumbled upon this quote from Frank Slootman (CEO of Snowflake), and I can’t stop thinking about it:

“Mediocrity is the silent killer.

Organizations are not getting killed by their C players. Everybody knows who they are, and performance eventually is addressed.

The people who kill organizations are your B players.

It’s the scourge of the enterprise because there are many and they are generally accepted.

Often, they are seen as not bad enough to fire, but not good enough to keep.

They are the ultimate passengers.

B players need to be pared:

They either become A players, or they become C players and get flushed out.

You can help by raising standards, by refusing mediocre outcomes.”

He put words to something I’ve felt for the past few years.

The clear underperformers on your team are always easy to let go.

But it’s the average middling performer:

The one with a decent attitude and okay performance who sticks around without making any major contributions.

And ultimately holds an organization back from growing.

This doesn’t mean every single position is going to be an A-player.

That’s unrealistic.

But you need to feel that your B-players are DEAD SET on leveling up and compensating for their skill deficiency with work ethic.

Otherwise, they are not going to ultimately perform at the level required.

And the longer you allow mediocrity to linger?

The more you communicate and reward a lower standard for everyone else.
posts.author: dickiebush
posts.summary: 組織や個人の成長を妨げる「Bプレイヤー」の存在と排除の重要性
posts.mind_theme: 卓越性の追求と基準の設定
key_insights: Bプレイヤーは組織の沈黙の殺人者である

中途半端なパフォーマンスの社員はAプレイヤーになるかCプレイヤーとして排除されるべき

posts.content:
8. You don't have to be an expert to write about something

In fact, people struggle to learn from experts (because they're too far ahead).

It's easier to learn from someone just 1 step ahead of you.

So start learning things and distilling them for those on the same path as you.

posts.title:
Money advice I would give my 22-year-old self:

Pick a topic, read about it every day, and write about it on X.

Actually first, delete Instagram from your phone.

It is poisoning your brain and makin
posts.content:
Money advice I would give my 22-year-old self:

Pick a topic, read about it every day, and write about it on X.

Actually first, delete Instagram from your phone.

It is poisoning your brain and making it harder for you to make money.

You can redownload Instagram once you’re living a life people actually find interesting to follow.

Once you’ve deleted it, replace the apps on your homescreen with Kindle & Audible.

These will be your new default activities: immersing yourself in the world of books on a certain topic you want to better understand.

But rather than just read and listen without a goal, you’re going to this material as the engine for your digital writing content.

Every day, you’re going to post one nugget you learned during your study that day. One framework, one tip, one lesson, one realization, something.

Doing this will lead to one of two outcomes:

1. Worst case, you better understand the concepts you’re learning since you have to write about them

2. Best case, you will meet like-minded people interested in this topic & begin to attract followers who want to learn alongside you

If you share one golden nugget per day every day for a year, you will have 365 pieces of content that are burned into your grey matter.

posts.title:
For whoever needs to hear it:

Smile more. There’s almost never a reason to go about your day in a bad mood. And to prove it, think about this: currently you would *cherish* the opportunity to live a
posts.content:
For whoever needs to hear it:

Smile more. There’s almost never a reason to go about your day in a bad mood. And to prove it, think about this: currently you would *cherish* the opportunity to live a day as younger you. And the younger you would *cherish* the opportunity to live a day as current you. And even current you — if you were sick or injured — would *cherish* the opportunity to live exactly as you are now. Take these reframes and remind yourself of them daily. You will feel more joy as a result.

posts.title: This is the most underrated productivity tool (and it costs $0):
posts.content:
This is the most underrated productivity tool (and it costs $0):                                                                                                                                                                                                                        

Daily walks in nature.

Since my mid-20s, I've been a step maxxer.

15-20k steps per day MINIMUM.

Walking should become your default activity if you are not actively producing something.

It's elite for your:

• Endorphins
• Body composition
• Creative thinking

The second I feel stagnant or low, I instantly drop everything and start walking. 

I could talk for hours about the positive impact taking an absurd number of daily steps has had on my life.

WALK MORE

On a walk is when 95% of my writing happens:

• Thinking
• Outlining
• Observing
• Brainstorming

The last 5% (when I actually sit down to type) comes later.

posts.title: inspired by @karpathy's LLM wiki idea, i've been building "the brain" - an LLM-maintained knowledge base that compiles raw sources into articles that surface where experts disagree rather than just su
posts.content:
inspired by @karpathy's LLM wiki idea, i've been building "the brain" - an LLM-maintained knowledge base that compiles raw sources into articles that surface where experts disagree rather than just summarising

pipeline: raw files (transcripts, courses, research) → extract specific claims → compile into trust-weighted reference articles. 

my own content = highest trust, sources defined as "mentors" 2nd highest (ie: people i trust who have good takes), "vetted" vs "unvetted" source material - vetted means i've read it and think it's great, unvetted = research from the internet i haven't yet explored in depth

LLM does the bookkeeping (@claudeai sonnet 4.6 seems just as good as opus 4.6 on this), i make the judgment calls on disagreements.

question i'm trying to answer: how do i actually make this useful? i'm working on a second book, we run a curriculum for our lifestyle business academy, i make youtube videos, we're building apps - can one knowledge base feed all of it? 

lots of reusable components - i've got 1000+ youtube videos i've made over the years, thousands of @readwise highlights, tonnes of notes from scientific papers when i was writing feel-good productivity (pre-LLM era)... surely there's a way for our new stuff to benefit from all this 

so far, the disagreement-surfacing is already the most interesting part. "researcher A says do X, person B says the opposite, here's the evidence for both" is way more useful than a clean summary when you're trying to figure out what to actually teach / write about 

i'll share more as it develops. if anyone's experimenting with something similar i'd love to hear what's working

posts.title:
An article called "something big is happening" blew up not too long ago. 

Written by a dev who builds AI for a living. About how we're no longer in the "this seems overblown" phase of AI and how it's
posts.content:
An article called "something big is happening" blew up not too long ago. 

Written by a dev who builds AI for a living. About how we're no longer in the "this seems overblown" phase of AI and how it's impacting businesses. 

He watched it happen to his own job first. 
AI building itself. 

The barrier to building a business just got unlocked.
The cost to build just collapsed.

The leverage now belongs to whoever knows what to build and who to build it for. And who can do it fastest.

That's the founder's unfair advantage right now.

It's the mass democratization of business scaling. The thing that used to take a whole team and millions of dollars can now be built by one kid in his garage. 

That should excited you. 

Because you, too, are the kid in the garage. 

The people writing those doomsday posts are grieving a world where having access to teams and funding was the moat around their business. 

Now taste is the moat. 
Your judgment is the moat. 

Recognize that masters of the old game always mistake the new one for a threat.

But the thing that's replacing that old game is way more exciting. 

Let's get after it.

gave my closer a pre-call brief generated by AI before every meeting.
 
it pulls the prospect's website, social, previous
posts.content:
Ran a sales experiment this month that instantly boosted my close rate 13%. 

gave my closer a pre-call brief generated by AI before every meeting.
 
it pulls the prospect's website, social, previous interactions, and creates a 1-page cheat sheet.

his close rate went from 38% to 51% in 3 weeks.

Proved that the meeting doesn't start when you join the call. it starts when you prep the call.

posts.title:
You can take things seriously without taking them personally.

Our tendency is to turn any criticism or complaint into a personal attack. We reply to it, defend against it, build a counter-argument, l
posts.content:
You can take things seriously without taking them personally.

Our tendency is to turn any criticism or complaint into a personal attack. We reply to it, defend against it, build a counter-argument, lose sleep over it.

You don't have to eat everything that is served to you. You can respond to criticism without digesting criticism. Take what's useful, do your best to improve, and leave the rest.

posts.content:
Exceptional people are rare. When you find someone wonderful, invest in them.

-When you find a great employee, pay them well. 

-When you find a great friend, prioritize the relationship.

Relationships are probably the most important part of life. Take care of the great ones.

People can help you in many ways throughout life, but there are two things nobody can give you: curiosity and drive. They must be self-supplied. 

If you are not interested and curious, all the inform
posts.content:
People can help you in many ways throughout life, but there are two things nobody can give you: curiosity and drive. They must be self-supplied. 

If you are not interested and curious, all the information in the world can be at your fingertips, but it will be relatively useless. If you are not motivated and driven, whatever connections or opportunities are available to you will be rendered inert.

Now, you won't feel curious and driven about every area of life, and that's fine. But it really pays to find something that lights you up. This is one of the primary quests of life: to find the thing that ignites your curiosity and drive. 

There are many recipes for success. There is no single way to win. But nearly all recipes include two ingredients: curiosity and drive.

posts.title:
Three ways to think about relationships:

-Deepening. You know each other better this year than last.

-Holding. Steady state, but mostly comfortable, familiar, and unchanged.

-Drifting. Contact is f
posts.content:
Three ways to think about relationships:

-Deepening. You know each other better this year than last.

-Holding. Steady state, but mostly comfortable, familiar, and unchanged.

-Drifting. Contact is fading and the relationship is starting to slide.

The point isn't to judge where you're at, but to notice what you need next. Which relationship is due for a deepening period? Which one are you okay letting drift for now?

A quick and easy tip for building habits that last:

Pick a standard time and place to do it.

It’s easier to wake up knowing “I exercise at 4 pm” than to decide each time when to fit a habit into your day.

If it’s already decided, all you need to do is show up.

posts.title:
In theory, consistency is about being disciplined, determined, and unwavering.

In practice, consistency is about being adaptable. Don't have much time? Scale it down. Don't have much energy? Do the e
posts.content:
In theory, consistency is about being disciplined, determined, and unwavering.

In practice, consistency is about being adaptable. Don't have much time? Scale it down. Don't have much energy? Do the easy version. Find different ways to show up depending on the circumstances. Let your habits change shape to meet the demands of the day.

Adaptability is the way of consistency.

posts.title:
Trades are always happening whether you see them or not. 

-Yes to the early meeting = no to the quiet morning

-Yes to the extra project = no to the free weekend

-Yes to something that drains you =
posts.content:
Trades are always happening whether you see them or not. 

-Yes to the early meeting = no to the quiet morning

-Yes to the extra project = no to the free weekend

-Yes to something that drains you = no to time that fulfills you

Saying yes to one thing is always saying no to something else. The cost of a bad yes isn't just the time it takes. It's whatever could have grown in that space instead.

The point isn't to say no to everything, but simply to recognize the difference between a good yes and a bad yes. Then, try to improve the ratio in your life.

posts.title:
Unexpected forms of generosity: 

-Being early can be a form of generosity. You wait, so they don't have to. 

-Leaving something unsaid can be a form of generosity. You don't always need the last wor
posts.content:
Unexpected forms of generosity: 

-Being early can be a form of generosity. You wait, so they don't have to. 

-Leaving something unsaid can be a form of generosity. You don't always need the last word. 

-Delivering your work on time can be a form of generosity. You make life easier for everyone downstream. 

-Not taking things personally can be a form of generosity. You give people the space to say things imperfectly.

posts.summary: 観察力のある人は多くの師を見つけることができる。
posts.mind_theme: 学習と観察
key_insights: 注意深く観察することで、あらゆるものから学びを得られる
師は特定の人物だけでなく、環境や出来事の中にも存在する
posts.target_audience: 学びを深めたい人、成長意欲のある人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
Trust in yourself is not only built through successful repetitions, but also through failed ones.

When you have worked through failures in the past, you fear them less in the future. You know you can
posts.content:
Trust in yourself is not only built through successful repetitions, but also through failed ones.

When you have worked through failures in the past, you fear them less in the future. You know you can bounce back.

Successful repetitions build competence. Failed repetitions build resilience.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 成功だけでなく、失敗経験も自己信頼と回復力を築く。
posts.mind_theme: 失敗からの学習と自己信頼
key_insights: 成功体験だけでなく、失敗体験も自己信頼を築く
過去の失敗を乗り越えることで、将来の失敗を恐れなくなる
成功は能力を、失敗は回復力を育む
posts.target_audience: 失敗を恐れる人、挑戦したいけど自信がない人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title: You are not your grand plans. You are your daily patterns.
posts.content: You are not your grand plans. You are your daily patterns.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 壮大な計画よりも日々の習慣がその人自身を形成する。
posts.mind_theme: 習慣の絶大な力
key_insights: 大きな計画よりも日々の小さな習慣が人間を作る
成功は日々のパターンによって決まる
posts.target_audience: 目標達成を目指す人、習慣化に興味がある人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
Ask yourself this question three times and try to refine and improve your answer each time: 

What do I really want?
posts.content:
Ask yourself this question three times and try to refine and improve your answer each time: 

What do I really want?

posts.mind_theme: 自己理解と目標設定
key_insights: 本当に欲しいものを何度も問い直し、答えを洗練させる
目標を明確にすることが行動の第一歩
posts.target_audience: 自分の目標を見つけたい人、自己理解を深めたい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
It only takes five minutes to break the cycle.

Five minutes of exercise and you are back on the path. Five minutes of writing and the manuscript is moving forward again. Five minutes of conversation
posts.content:
It only takes five minutes to break the cycle.

Five minutes of exercise and you are back on the path. Five minutes of writing and the manuscript is moving forward again. Five minutes of conversation and the relationship is restored.

It doesn't take much to feel good again.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: たった5分で悪いサイクルを断ち切り、良い流れに戻すことができる。
posts.mind_theme: 行動のきっかけと習慣化
key_insights: たった5分で悪いサイクルを断ち切り、良い方向に転換できる
少量の行動でも、始めることで気分が良くなる
posts.target_audience: 行動に移せない人、気分転換したい人、習慣を再開したい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
In the gym, if you experience no stimulus your muscles won't grow. If you step under 10,000 pounds your body will break. 

Life is similar. 

Too much challenge makes life hard, but so does too little
posts.content:
In the gym, if you experience no stimulus your muscles won't grow. If you step under 10,000 pounds your body will break. 

Life is similar. 

Too much challenge makes life hard, but so does too little.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 人生における挑戦のバランスの重要性。
posts.mind_theme: 適度な挑戦と成長
key_insights: 人生は適度な挑戦が必要であり、少なすぎても多すぎても成長できない
筋肉と同様に、適度な負荷が成長を促す
posts.target_audience: 成長が停滞していると感じる人、過度なストレスに悩む人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
A Monday morning question for you: 

Is the situation actually complicated or is it really quite straightforward, but you're making it complicated because it requires a lot of courage to make the stra
posts.content:
A Monday morning question for you: 

Is the situation actually complicated or is it really quite straightforward, but you're making it complicated because it requires a lot of courage to make the straightforward decision?
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 状況はシンプルなのに、勇気がなくて複雑にしているだけかもしれない。
posts.mind_theme: 決断力と勇気
key_insights: 複雑に見える状況も、実はシンプルな決断を必要としているだけかもしれない

posts.target_audience: 決断を先延ばしにする人、問題解決に悩む人

posts.title: Don't spend too much time around someone whose primary way of engaging with life is as a martyr. When suffering becomes your identity, you only see the hard parts of life.
posts.content: Don't spend too much time around someone whose primary way of engaging with life is as a martyr. When suffering becomes your identity, you only see the hard parts of life.

posts.summary: 苦しみをアイデンティティにする人から距離を置く重要性。
posts.mind_theme: 人間関係と自己認識
key_insights: 常に苦しみを語る人と関わりすぎない
苦しみを自己のアイデンティティにすると、人生の良い側面が見えなくなる
posts.target_audience: 人間関係で悩む人、ネガティブな影響を受けやすい人

posts.title:
If the path is crowded, differentiate.

If the path is empty, validate.
posts.content:
If the path is crowded, differentiate.

If the path is empty, validate.

posts.summary: 混雑した道では差別化し、空いている道では検証する
posts.mind_theme: 成長戦略と差別化の思考法
key_insights: 競合が多い分野では独自性を出す
未開拓分野では仮説検証を重視する
posts.target_audience: SNSで差別化を図りたい人、ビジネス戦略を考えたい人

posts.title:
How to see opportunities others miss:

1) Study a totally different field, then return to the original problem. Apply insights from other domains.

2) Invert the problem. Try to achieve the opposite.
posts.content:
How to see opportunities others miss:

1) Study a totally different field, then return to the original problem. Apply insights from other domains.

2) Invert the problem. Try to achieve the opposite.

3) Find ways to engage with hyper-creative people. Their thinking will rub off.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 人が見逃す機会を見つけるための思考法を3つ紹介
posts.mind_theme: イノベーションを生む多角的思考
key_insights: 異なる分野から知見を応用する
問題を逆転させて考える
創造的な人々との交流を増やす
posts.target_audience: 新しいアイデアを探す人、問題解決能力を高めたい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
Always ask, but never expect.

Always ask for what you want. Many people are happy to help—if the request is direct and specific. In a surprising number of cases, something remarkable is possible if y
posts.content:
Always ask, but never expect.

Always ask for what you want. Many people are happy to help—if the request is direct and specific. In a surprising number of cases, something remarkable is possible if you have the courage to ask.

Never expect people to say yes. Everyone is busy and balancing multiple priorities. Your request is not their responsibility. When you're told no, move on lightly and freely. The world is full of opportunity.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 常に要求するが、期待はしないという行動原則
posts.mind_theme: 目標達成と人間関係におけるマインドセット
key_insights: 具体的な要求は助けを得る可能性を高める
断られても次へ進む軽やかさを持つ
posts.target_audience: 自己主張が苦手な人、チャンスを掴みたい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title: You don’t always get to choose the load, but you can choose how to carry it.
posts.content: You don’t always get to choose the load, but you can choose how to carry it.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 人生の困難な状況でも、受け止め方を選ぶことの重要性
posts.mind_theme: 逆境におけるマインドセット
key_insights: 負担は選べなくても、担ぎ方は選べる
状況にどう対応するかは自分次第
posts.target_audience: 困難に直面している人、精神的な強さを求める人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
In the modern world, it is easy to feel like a passenger: reacting to notifications, responding to demands, consuming whatever you happen to drive past on your screen.

But joy is found in being the d
posts.content:
In the modern world, it is easy to feel like a passenger: reacting to notifications, responding to demands, consuming whatever you happen to drive past on your screen.

But joy is found in being the driver. It's the act of looking at the raw material of your circumstances — your time, your energy, your relationships, your skills — and seeing what you can make from it.

It is the act of creating the life you want (in big and small ways) that makes you feel alive and imbues life with extra meaning. The fact that you can hold a vision in your mind and then, however imperfectly, bend reality a few degrees in that direction.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 人生の運転手となり、自ら創造することの喜びと意味
posts.mind_theme: 人生の主導権を握るマインドセット
key_insights: 受動的ではなく能動的に生きる
自分の時間やスキルで人生を創造する喜び
posts.target_audience: 人生の目的を見失いがちな人、主体性を高めたい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
You should always be rooting for the people you know. Not only because you may need their support tomorrow, but also because it feels good to celebrate something.

Celebration can rescue your day—even
posts.content:
You should always be rooting for the people you know. Not only because you may need their support tomorrow, but also because it feels good to celebrate something.

Celebration can rescue your day—even if it is someone else's victory. Envy will ruin your day—even if you're actually winning.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 他者の成功を祝い、嫉妬を避けることの重要性
posts.mind_theme: 人間関係と幸福感のマインドセット
key_insights: 他者を応援することは自分自身の喜びになる
嫉妬は自分自身を苦しめる
posts.target_audience: 人間関係で悩む人、ポジティブな精神状態を保ちたい人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
A Monday morning question for you: 

The cost of convenience is vitality. You can have a lot of things done for you in the modern world — and many of them are great time savers! — but the feeling of b
posts.content:
A Monday morning question for you: 

The cost of convenience is vitality. You can have a lot of things done for you in the modern world — and many of them are great time savers! — but the feeling of being alive comes from being fully engaged in the right task, not free from all tasks.

Sure, you can automate and delegate all sorts of things, but the more interesting question is, "What fills you with so much liveliness that you want to do the work yourself?"
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 利便性の代償と、活動的であることの価値を問う
posts.mind_theme: 生産性と生きがいに関する思考法
key_insights: 利便性は活力を奪うことがある
真の生きがいは、自分が深く関わる仕事から生まれる
posts.target_audience: 効率化と充実感のバランスを探る人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
Environment can multiply (or divide) your effort.

Before you plant the garden, study the soil. After you plant the garden, tend the weeds.

First, selection. Think carefully about the right person to
posts.content:
Environment can multiply (or divide) your effort.

Before you plant the garden, study the soil. After you plant the garden, tend the weeds.

First, selection. Think carefully about the right person to marry or business to pursue or city to live in.

Then, cultivation. Once you choose, care for it lovingly and nurture it into the best it can be.
posts.author: JamesClear
posts.summary: 環境が努力を増幅（または減少）させるという考え方
posts.mind_theme: 成功に繋がる環境選択と育成の思考法
key_insights: 努力の前に環境（土壌）を慎重に選ぶ
一度選んだら愛情を込めて育てる
posts.target_audience: キャリア選択や人間関係で悩む人、持続的な成長を望む人
posts.credibility: フォロワー120万人超のベストセラー著者

posts.title:
Many situations in life are similar to going on a hike: the view changes once you start walking.

You don't need all the answers right now. New paths will reveal themselves if you have the courage to
posts.content:
Many situations in life are similar to going on a hike: the view changes once you start walking.

You don't need all the answers right now. New paths will reveal themselves if you have the courage to get started.

posts.summary: 始めてみれば、新たな道や解決策が見つかるという行動原則

key_insights: すべてを把握していなくても、まず行動することが重要

posts.title: It's remarkable how often the real problem is not what happened, but how it was communicated.
